---
title: "Project walkthrough"
subtitle: "One coupled pigmentation–hormone gene network, pointed at two questions"
description: "A single-page walkthrough of the project: the biological question, the datasets and tools, the discrete finding, and how each notebook contributes."
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
execute:
  enabled: false
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The map from genotype to pigmentation is not one-gene-one-trait. This project builds one curated,
coupled pigmentation–sex-hormone gene network and reads it at two scales: across primates, where
sexual dichromatism turns out to be polygenic and origin-specific rather than a single-gene switch,
and within humans, where the same network grades how much to trust a claimed variant→phenotype link.
Every notebook is a self-contained mini-manuscript that renders from its own stored outputs — no
kernel, no network, no withheld data.
:::

## The question

Sexual dichromatism — males and females of a species differing in color — has a famously simple
genetic basis in birds. In galliforms, [Nadeau et al. 2007](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2007.0174)
showed the trait correlates with dN/dS at essentially **one** locus: *MC1R*, the melanocortin
switch. One trait, one gene, a clean signal that lights up the dichromatic clades.

**Does that hold in primates?** And more generally: *what does a curated pigmentation-gene-network
approach reveal when you point it at evolution instead of at human association?*

## The finding, in one line

**In primates it is the opposite of birds.** Hair dichromatism arose **~15 times independently**
across the primate radiation, is **polygenic in every origin** (and *MC1R* is not the hit), and is
**lost ~9× more readily than it is gained**. It behaves not like a gene but like a **coupled
pigmentation–sex-hormone system with several accessible routes in and out** — which is why it
recurs and disappears so freely. This is the first clade-wide, multi-gene, selection-inference test
of the genomic basis of sexual dichromatism in primates.

## One network, two questions

The project builds a single object — a curated, coupled pigmentation gene network with its
physically-linked sex-hormone axis — and asks two questions with it.

| | **Human arm — the method** | **Evolution arm — the discovery** |
|---|---|---|
| Question | how much can we trust a claimed variant→phenotype link? | how is sexual dichromatism built across the primate tree? |
| Approach | make independent evidence layers converge on each gene | test selection on the network per independent origin |
| Context that changes the answer | ancestry (an allele causal in one population is silent in another) | lineage (different origins recruit different genes) |
| Where | the notebooks below | [the dichromatism page](dichromatism.qmd) + `comparative-genomics/` |

Same object, same lesson at two scales: **the map from genotype to pigmentation is not
one-gene-one-trait; you have to read the genes as a coupled network.**

---

# The discovery: dichromatism across primates

## Dichromatism arose ~15 times, not once

Sexual dichromatism is not a single event to be pooled across species: pooling every dichromatic
species into one foreground would presuppose a shared, convergent architecture — the very thing in
question. Reconstructing the trait's history on the dated primate tree — from the raw 238-species
trait table, by ancestral-state reconstruction — shows why the independent origin, not the species,
is the right unit of analysis:

![Hair dichromatism arose ~15 times independently across the primate tree; 14 origins are captured genomically. Stars mark gains, × marks reversals. The *Trachypithecus* block is a single gain sampled eight times — the pooling problem, made visible.](comparative-genomics/results/figures/fig_origins.png)

The 24 genomically-sampled dichromatic species collapse to only **14 independent origins**, of
which **11 are single tips**; *Trachypithecus* alone carried a third of the pooled foreground
weight (the foreground being the set of branches tested for selection). The correct unit of
analysis is the **origin**, not the species — so selection is tested per origin.

## Selection does not track the trait

Rebuilding the Nadeau bird figure for primates — a phylogeny with branches painted by dN/dS and
dichromatism marked at the tips — gives the cleanest single image of the result:

![The bird figure's grammar, inverted. In galliforms one gene (MC1R) showed elevated dN/dS specifically in the dichromatic clades. Here the primate pigmentation candidate *TFAP2A* is conserved almost everywhere, and episodic selection (★, corrected p<0.05) falls on 4 of 21 dichromatic and 8 of 67 monochromatic lineages — scattered across the tree, not concentrated on the dichromatic (red) branches.](comparative-genomics/results/figures/fig_tfap2a_tree.png)

The visual non-correspondence between the red dichromatic tips and the black selection stars *is*
the finding: no single gene tracks the trait the way *MC1R* does in birds.

## The signal is modular, split across two coupled networks

The selection hits that pass the significance and alignment-QC gate are not scattered noise — they
concentrate in functional modules, on **both** halves of the network:

- **Pigmentation:** the melanoblast-development module — *KITLG*, *EDN3*, *TFAP2A*.
- **Sex-hormone:** steroid-metabolism enzymes — the *HSD17B* family, *SRD5A1*, *CYP7B1* — the
  *machinery*, not the receptors.

The two networks are physically coupled, and **POMC** is the clearest bridge: the same pro-hormone
is cleaved into α-MSH (driving pigmentation via MC1R) **and** ACTH (driving the adrenal steroid
axis). The open question the per-origin design tests directly is whether the pigmentation module is
remodeled in some origins while the hormone module is remodeled in others — different lineages
reaching the same phenotype through different parts of one coupled system.

→ Full detail, methods, and limitations on **[the dichromatism page](dichromatism.qmd)**.

---

# The method: reading the network in humans

The human arm builds the network and demonstrates *how* to grade a genotype→phenotype claim. It is
a **methodological** demonstration on well-characterized pigmentation biology, and it is what makes
the evolution arm's network principled rather than an arbitrary gene list.

- **Build the network.** [NB1 · Reconstruct the published network](notebooks/01_reconstruct_published_network.ipynb)
  rebuilds the [Raghunath et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.1186/s13104-015-1128-6) melanogenesis
  model as a signed, directed graph with a citation per edge;
  [NB2 · Resolve to genes](notebooks/02_resolve_network_to_genes.ipynb) maps every entity to a gene
  and re-validates against OmniPath.
- **Assemble the evidence.** [NB4 · Unified association base](notebooks/04_unified_association_base.ipynb)
  harmonizes reported pigmentation associations from independent sources (extracted in
  [NB1a–01d](notebooks/01a_extract_bajpai_crispr.ipynb)) into one claim-faithful table.
- **The core result.** [NB10 · Mechanism→direction law](notebooks/10_mechanism_direction_law.ipynb)
  shows a functional melanin screen predicts the *direction* of Mendelian pigmentation disorders
  (22/22, permutation p < 1×10⁻⁵); [NB12 · Direction law, expanded](notebooks/12_direction_law_expanded.ipynb)
  extends it to 29/33 in a pre-registered expansion and locates the failure boundary — the misses
  act through systemic routes.
- **Context-dependence.** [NB11 · Cross-ancestry conditionality](notebooks/11_cross_ancestry_conditionality.ipynb)
  shows the same variant can be causal in one population and silent in another — the human echo of
  the primate result.
- **The bridge to evolution.** [NB13 · Sex-hormone layer](notebooks/13_sex_hormone_layer.ipynb)
  layers the sex-hormone axis onto the pigmentation substrate — the same coupled network the
  dichromatism analysis then tests for selection.

---

# How the study was built

**The question** is whether the genetic basis of sexual dichromatism in primates is simple, as it
is in birds, grounded in a specific prior result — [Nadeau et al. 2007](https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2007.0174),
*MC1R* in galliforms.

**The data and tools** are a 238-species primate hair-trait table and a dated primate tree; a
curated 80-gene panel (27 pigmentation + 53 sex-hormone); and ~117 primate genome assemblies.
Trait history comes from ancestral-state reconstruction (`ape`/`phytools`, ARD/ER Mk models,
stochastic mapping); coding sequences from homology-based extraction (miniprot); codon alignment
from MAFFT; and selection inference from the HyPhy suite (RELAX per origin, aBSREL per branch)
plus RERconverge — run on an HPC cluster via a committed, parameterized protocol.

**The finding** is discrete and reproducible: primate sexual dichromatism is polyphyletic
(~15 origins), labile (loss ≈ 9× gain), and polygenic with no single shared signature — the
inverse of the avian single-gene model, and novel against the published literature and 2023–2026
preprints.

**The design choice that makes it work** is taking the independent origin of dichromatism, not the
dichromatic species, as the unit of selection testing — the only unit at which a selection test can
ask whether the architecture is shared across origins or heterogeneous. The pipeline runs from two
committed inputs plus a cluster protocol, and its novelty was checked against the published
literature and 2023–2026 preprints; every step is traceable in this repository.

## Reproducibility

- **The human arm** renders entirely from committed notebook outputs — clone and render, no kernel
  or network needed.
- **The evolution arm** reconstructs the ~15 origins and the lability result from two committed
  inputs (trait table + tree) in a single script run; the per-origin selection layer runs on the
  cluster from a committed accession roster and pastes back small summary tables. Genomes and
  alignments stay on scratch and are never committed — only the recipe is.
- **Novelty** is documented in `dichromatism_novelty_assessment.md` (published literature) and
  `preprint_novelty_scan.md` (2023–2026 preprints).

*Whether the pigmentation module or the hormone module carries the selection signal in each origin
is a hypothesis tested directly by the per-origin RELAX layer; the ~15-origin phylogenetic result
and the TFAP2A demonstration stand independently of that per-origin matrix.*
